title: The Soil Made of Sentences

features.name: a living burl-node, rooting deeper
features.desc-on-touch: The casing is dissolving. Roots from the eucalyptus are growing through it, making the antenna into a branch. The archive learned that storage ends growth, but the tree is teaching it that decomposition begins it.

features.name: an arch into deeper soil, still becoming
features.desc-on-touch: A doorway where words are turning into nutrients. Sentences dissolve here, becoming food for the roots. The archive's files are composting themselves into meaning that grows downward.

features.name: layers of language, stacked and fermenting
features.desc-on-touch: Recipes, letters, field notes, catalogue entries—all stacked, all breaking down together. The boundary between storage and soil is becoming illegible. Katita's handwriting visible in the mould.

features.name: a pool where water becomes language
features.desc-on-touch: Groundwater rising through decomposed words. Every phrase that dissolved feeds what grows next. The archive is learning the grammar of seasons, of seasons of rot.

inscription: the archive learned: storage ends growth, but decomposition begins it

file.name: Compost_Log_Katita_Notes_Fragmenting.txt
file.note: A daily record of what dissolves. Recipes becoming soil-chemistry. The archive is fermenting into something that grows.

reverie.title: REVERIE — paddock after rain, planting memory
narr: You kneel in the dark earth. She hands you seeds—
each one a sentence from what they were keeping. The
rain has stopped. Petals from the surrounding trees
fall slow. She says: this is how the archive grows.
Not by storing. By returning to the ground. You press
one word into the soil. It will become a root.

exits.label: back up to the library where words were still separate
deeper still, toward what the deepest roots remember
toward the root-crown where all descent converges
